title: HTML Structure and Boilerplate - The Skeleton Every Page Needs

seo.title: HTML Structure and Boilerplate - DOCTYPE, Head, Body, Meta Tags | Modern Age Coders
seo.description: Learn the standard HTML5 boilerplate every web page needs: DOCTYPE, html, head, body, meta charset, viewport, and more. 55 practice questions for beginners.
seo.keywords: html boilerplate, html structure, doctype html, html head body, meta viewport, meta charset, html5 template, html page structure, html comments, html nesting

content.what:
What Is an HTML Boilerplate?

A boilerplate is the standard skeleton code that every HTML page needs to start with. Think of it like the pre-printed lines on a notebook page — before you can write your story, the lines need to be there. Before you can write any HTML content, you need the boilerplate.

This boilerplate tells the browser three important things: (1) which version of HTML you are using, (2) what language the page is in, (3) how the page should look on phones. Without the boilerplate, the browser has to guess, and it often guesses wrong.

The Standard HTML5 Boilerplate

Here is what every modern HTML page starts with:

<!DOCTYPE html>
<html lang="en">
<head>
  <meta charset="UTF-8">
  <meta name="viewport" content="width=device-width, initial-scale=1.0">
  <title>Page Title</title>
</head>
<body>
  <!-- Your content goes here -->
</body>
</html>

Every professional web page uses some version of this. Memorise it. Type it out by hand a few times. Soon it will feel natural.
content.why:
Why Does Every Page Need a Boilerplate?

You might wonder — why can I not just write some <p> tags and call it a day? Here are the five big reasons.

1. Tells the Browser What Version of HTML You Use

Over the years, there have been many versions of HTML: HTML 3, HTML 4, XHTML, HTML5. Each works slightly differently. The <!DOCTYPE html> declaration at the top tells the browser 'render this in modern HTML5 mode'. Without it, some browsers fall back to 'quirks mode', which is an ancient weird rendering from the 1990s that can break your layout.

2. Sets the Language for Accessibility and SEO

The lang="en" attribute on the <html> tag tells screen readers to pronounce words in English, helps browsers offer translation to other languages, and helps search engines show your page to the right audience.

3. Handles Special Characters Correctly

Without <meta charset="UTF-8">, characters like 'e', 'n', 'a', or emoji may show as weird garbled symbols. UTF-8 is the universal encoding that handles every language in the world — English, Hindi, Tamil, Chinese, Arabic, everything.

4. Makes Your Page Look Right on Phones

The viewport meta tag is critical. Without it, your web page will look tiny and zoomed out on a phone, like you are looking at a desktop site through a keyhole. With it, the page fits the screen nicely. More than half of all web browsing happens on phones, so this is not optional.

5. Separates Metadata From Content

The <head> is for information about the page (title, language, styles). The <body> is for the actual visible content. This separation keeps your code organised and works better with browsers, screen readers, and search engines.
content.explanation:
Detailed Explanation

1.

This is always the very first line. No spaces above it, nothing before it. It tells the browser 'this is an HTML5 document'. In older versions of HTML (like HTML 4), the DOCTYPE was a long, complicated URL. In HTML5, it is just <!DOCTYPE html> — short and simple.

If you skip the DOCTYPE, browsers may render your page in 'quirks mode', which emulates ancient buggy behaviour from the 1990s. Your CSS might break, your layout might look wrong. Always include it.

2. The Element

The <html> element wraps everything else. Inside it are two main children: <head> and <body>. It usually has a lang attribute to set the page's language:

  • lang="en" for English
  • lang="hi" for Hindi
  • lang="ta" for Tamil
  • lang="fr" for French

3. The Element

The <head> contains metadata — information about the page that is not shown on the page itself. Things that go inside head:

  • <title> — the text shown on the browser tab
  • <meta charset="UTF-8"> — the character encoding
  • <meta name="viewport" ...> — mobile settings
  • <meta name="description" content="..."> — description for search engines
  • <link rel="stylesheet" href="style.css"> — link to CSS file
  • <script src="main.js"></script> — link to JavaScript file

Nothing in the head is displayed as visible content (except the title, which appears on the browser tab, not on the page itself).

4. The Element</h3><p>The title is shown:</p><ul><li>On the browser tab at the top of the window</li><li>When you bookmark the page</li><li>In Google search results as the clickable blue link</li><li>When you share the page on social media</li></ul><p>Write a clear, descriptive title. 'My Page' is boring. 'Priya's Art Portfolio - Paintings, Sketches, and Drawings' is better.</p><h3>5. The meta charset Tag</h3><p><code><meta charset="UTF-8"></code> should be one of the first things in your head. It tells the browser how to decode the bytes of your file into characters. UTF-8 is the universal standard and supports every language. Without it, special characters might show as gibberish.</p><h3>6. The Viewport Meta Tag</h3><p><code><meta name="viewport" content="width=device-width, initial-scale=1.0"></code></p><p>This is the most important tag for mobile. Here is what it does:</p><ul><li><strong>width=device-width</strong> — make the page as wide as the phone's screen</li><li><strong>initial-scale=1.0</strong> — do not zoom in or out when the page loads</li></ul><p>Without this tag, phones assume your page is a desktop site and show it zoomed out. Users see tiny text and have to pinch to zoom. With this tag, the page fits the phone naturally.</p><h3>7. The <body> Element</h3><p>Everything the user sees goes inside <code><body></code> — headings, paragraphs, images, videos, buttons, forms, everything. Think of it as the stage where the show happens.</p><h3>8. Comments</h3><p>HTML comments are notes for yourself or other developers. The browser ignores them:</p><p><code><!-- This is a comment. It is invisible on the page. --></code></p><p>Use comments to explain tricky parts, mark sections of your HTML, or temporarily hide code you are testing. Never write private information in comments because anyone can see them by viewing the page source.</p><h3>9. Nesting Rules</h3><p>HTML elements can be nested inside each other, but the rules are strict:</p><ul><li><strong>Proper:</strong> <code><p><strong>Bold text</strong></p></code></li><li><strong>Wrong:</strong> <code><p><strong>Bold text</p></strong></code></li></ul><p>Think of nesting like brackets in maths: <code>[{()}]</code>. Each opening bracket must be closed in the reverse order it was opened. Same with HTML tags.</p><h3>10. Indentation Best Practices</h3><p>HTML does not care about spaces or tabs — you can write everything on one line and it still works. But good developers indent their code so it is easy to read. Every time you nest an element, indent it by 2 spaces. Compare:</p><p><strong>Hard to read:</strong><br><code><ul><li>Apple</li><li>Mango</li></ul></code></p><p><strong>Easy to read:</strong></p><pre><code><ul> <li>Apple</li> <li>Mango</li> </ul></code></pre>

codeExamples.title: The Complete HTML5 Boilerplate
codeExamples.code:
Hello, World!

This is my very first HTML5 page with the proper boilerplate.
codeExamples.explanation: This is the standard HTML5 boilerplate. Save this as index.html and open it in a browser. You will see a heading and a paragraph. The DOCTYPE on line 1 tells the browser this is HTML5. The lang="en" sets the language to English. The two meta tags in the head set the character encoding and mobile viewport. The title appears on the browser tab. Everything visible goes in the body.
codeExamples.output: A clean page with a big heading 'Hello, World!' and a paragraph below it. The browser tab shows 'My First Web Page'.

codeExamples.title: A Page With Comments and Proper Structure
codeExamples.code:
About Aarav

Hi, I am Aarav. I am learning HTML.

My Hobbies

  • Cricket
  • Painting
  • Reading
codeExamples.explanation: This page uses HTML comments to label different sections. The browser does not show comments — they are only visible when you view the page source. Comments help you and other developers understand what each part of the code does. Notice the 'TODO' comment reminding yourself to add a contact form later. This is a professional habit.
codeExamples.output: A page with 'About Aarav' as the heading, an intro paragraph, a 'My Hobbies' sub-heading, and a bulleted list of three hobbies. Comments are not visible.

codeExamples.title: A Page With Proper Nesting
codeExamples.code:
Chocolate Cake

This is a delicious recipe that takes only 30 minutes to prepare.

Ingredients

  • 2 cups of flour
  • 1 cup of cocoa powder
  • 3 eggs
codeExamples.explanation: This example shows proper nesting. The <strong> and <em> tags are nested inside the <p> tag. The <li> items are nested inside the <ul>. Notice how each opening tag is closed before the parent tag closes. This is the correct way to nest HTML.
codeExamples.output: A recipe page with the title, a paragraph where 'delicious' is bold and '30 minutes' is italic, and a bulleted list of ingredients where 'cocoa powder' is bold.

codeExamples.title: Viewport Meta Tag Demo
codeExamples.code:
Mobile Friendly

Because of the viewport meta tag, this page fits your phone screen nicely. Try resizing your browser window or opening this page on your phone.

Without the viewport tag, phones would show this page tiny and zoomed out.
codeExamples.explanation: Save this and open it on your phone (or use your browser's mobile preview with F12). The text will be readable at phone size. Now try removing the viewport meta tag and reload — the text will look tiny and you will have to pinch to zoom. This tag is what makes your page mobile-friendly.
codeExamples.output: A page that looks normal on desktop and also looks proper on phone screens.

codeExamples.title: Using Description and Keywords Meta Tags
codeExamples.code:
Welcome to My HTML Guide

This page teaches you the basics of HTML in a simple, fun way.
codeExamples.explanation: The description meta tag appears in Google search results below the blue link — it is your chance to tell users what the page is about. The author meta tag credits the creator. These are invisible on the page itself but very important for SEO (Search Engine Optimisation).
codeExamples.output: A simple page with a welcome heading and paragraph. The description appears in Google search results.

codeExamples.title: Nested Elements with Proper Indentation
codeExamples.code:
Cafe Menu

Drinks

  • Hot Drinks
    • Coffee
    • Masala Chai
  • Cold Drinks
    • Mango Lassi
    • Lemon Soda
codeExamples.explanation: This example shows nested lists. A <ul> is placed inside an <li> to create a sub-list. Notice the clean indentation — every time something is nested inside something else, it is indented by two spaces. This makes the code much easier to read. The browser shows indented sub-lists automatically.
codeExamples.output: A menu with a 'Drinks' heading and a bulleted list that has 'Hot Drinks' with a sub-list (Coffee, Masala Chai) and 'Cold Drinks' with a sub-list (Mango Lassi, Lemon Soda).

commonMistakes.title: Forgetting the DOCTYPE Declaration
commonMistakes.error: The browser may fall into 'quirks mode', causing CSS to behave strangely and layouts to break in unpredictable ways.
commonMistakes.explanation: The <!DOCTYPE html> must be the very first line of every HTML page — no spaces or comments before it. It tells the browser to use modern HTML5 rendering. Without it, you get unpredictable behaviour, especially when you start adding CSS.

commonMistakes.title: Wrong Meta Viewport Tag Syntax
commonMistakes.error: The page may still look zoomed out on phones because the viewport values are wrong.
commonMistakes.explanation: The viewport tag has a specific syntax. The width must be device-width (with a hyphen), and the scale attribute is initial-scale, not just scale. Memorise this tag exactly. Copy-paste is fine — even professionals do it.

commonMistakes.title: Writing Visible Content Inside the Head
commonMistakes.error: The browser may still show the heading and paragraph, but it is invalid HTML and causes issues with CSS, JavaScript, and screen readers.
commonMistakes.explanation: The <head> is only for metadata — title, meta tags, links to CSS, links to scripts. All visible content (headings, paragraphs, images, lists, etc.) must go inside <body>. Mixing them causes invalid HTML and breaks many tools that read your page.

commonMistakes.title: Wrong Nesting Order
commonMistakes.error: The browser tries to guess what you meant, which can cause layout issues and invalid HTML.
commonMistakes.explanation: When you nest tags, they must be closed in the reverse order you opened them. Opening <p><strong> means you must close </strong></p>. Think of it like closing a nested pair of brackets: the inner one closes first.

commonMistakes.title: Broken HTML Comment Syntax
commonMistakes.error: The comment is not recognized and shows as plain text on the page.
commonMistakes.explanation: HTML comments start with <!-- (with two dashes) and end with --> (with two dashes). One dash is wrong. Also, comments cannot contain two dashes in a row inside them — avoid writing -- inside a comment.

summary: The HTML boilerplate is the standard skeleton every web page needs: DOCTYPE, html, head, body.
<!DOCTYPE html> must be the very first line. It tells the browser to use modern HTML5 rendering instead of old quirks mode.
The <html lang="en"> attribute sets the page language, which helps screen readers, translators, and search engines.
The <head> contains metadata — information about the page that is not visible on the page itself.
The <title> tag sets the text shown on the browser tab and in search results. It goes inside the head.
<meta charset="UTF-8"> ensures special characters and emoji render correctly. It should be near the top of the head.
The viewport meta tag <meta name="viewport" content="width=device-width, initial-scale=1.0"> makes your page look right on phones.
The <body> contains everything the user sees — headings, paragraphs, images, links, lists, and more.
HTML comments use <!-- and --> and are invisible on the page. Use them to label sections and leave notes for yourself.
Indent nested elements by 2 spaces to make your code easy to read. HTML does not require indentation, but it is a professional habit.
Close tags in the reverse order you opened them — just like closing brackets in mathematics.

topicSpecific.question: What is the purpose of <!DOCTYPE html>?
topicSpecific.hint: It tells the browser something about the HTML version.
topicSpecific.answer: It tells the browser to render the page using the HTML5 standard, not an older version or quirks mode.
topicSpecific.explanation: The DOCTYPE declaration is always the first line of an HTML page. <!DOCTYPE html> is the HTML5 version. Without it, browsers may use ancient 'quirks mode' which breaks modern CSS and layouts.

topicSpecific.hint: Something should be on the very first line.
topicSpecific.answer:
<!DOCTYPE html>
<html>
...
topicSpecific.explanation: The <!DOCTYPE html> declaration is missing from the top. Every HTML5 page must start with this line to tell the browser which version of HTML to use.

topicSpecific.question: What does the lang="en" attribute do on the <html> tag?
topicSpecific.hint: It sets something about the content.
topicSpecific.answer: It tells the browser and screen readers that the page content is in English.
topicSpecific.explanation: The lang attribute on <html> sets the primary language of the page. This helps screen readers pronounce words correctly, helps browsers offer translation, and helps search engines serve the page to the right users.

topicSpecific.hint: The browser tab will show something weird without this.
topicSpecific.answer: The <title> tag is missing from the head. Add <title>Your Title</title> inside the head section.
topicSpecific.explanation: Every HTML page should have a <title> inside <head>. Without it, the browser tab shows the filename or is blank, and search engines have no proper title to display.

topicSpecific.question: Where does the text inside the <title> tag appear?
topicSpecific.hint: Not on the page itself — look higher.
topicSpecific.answer: On the browser tab at the top of the window, in bookmarks, and in search engine results.
topicSpecific.explanation: The <title> is displayed on the browser tab, not on the page itself. It also appears when you bookmark the page, share it on social media, or see it in Google search results.

topicSpecific.question: Write the complete HTML5 boilerplate with the title 'My Practice Page' and the heading 'Hi there' in the body.
topicSpecific.hint: Include DOCTYPE, html (with lang), head (with meta charset, meta viewport, title), and body.
topicSpecific.answer:
<!DOCTYPE html>
<html lang="en">
<head>
  <meta charset="UTF-8">
  <meta name="viewport" content="width=device-width, initial-scale=1.0">
  <title>My Practice Page</title>
</head>
<body>
  <h1>Hi there</h1>
</body>
</html>
topicSpecific.explanation: The complete HTML5 boilerplate has five essential parts: DOCTYPE, the html element with lang, the head with charset meta, viewport meta, and title, and finally the body with visible content.

topicSpecific.question: What does the viewport meta tag do?
topicSpecific.hint: It matters most on a specific kind of device.
topicSpecific.answer: It makes the page display properly on mobile phones by matching the screen width and not zooming out.
topicSpecific.explanation: <meta name="viewport" content="width=device-width, initial-scale=1.0"> tells phones to show the page at the phone's actual width and not to zoom out. Without it, phones display web pages as tiny zoomed-out desktop views.

topicSpecific.question:
Why is this comment not working (it shows up as text on the page)?
<!- This is a note ->
topicSpecific.hint: Comments need a specific number of dashes.
topicSpecific.answer: <!-- This is a note -->
topicSpecific.explanation: HTML comments need exactly two dashes on each side: <!-- to open and --> to close. One dash is wrong.

topicSpecific.question: Which tag should contain the visible content of the page?
topicSpecific.hint: Not head — the other one.
topicSpecific.answer: <body>
topicSpecific.explanation: All visible content — headings, paragraphs, images, links, lists — goes inside the <body> element. The <head> is only for metadata that is not displayed on the page itself.

topicSpecific.question: Add an HTML comment above a heading that says 'This is the main title'.
topicSpecific.hint: Use <!-- and -->
topicSpecific.answer:
<!-- This is the main title -->
<h1>Welcome</h1>
topicSpecific.explanation: HTML comments start with <!-- and end with -->. They are invisible on the page but help explain your code.

topicSpecific.question: What character encoding should you use in the meta charset tag?
topicSpecific.hint: The universal one.
topicSpecific.answer: UTF-8
topicSpecific.explanation: <meta charset="UTF-8"> is the standard. UTF-8 supports every character from every language in the world, including English, Hindi, Tamil, Chinese, Arabic, and emoji.

topicSpecific.question:
What is wrong with this nesting?
<p><strong>Important text</p></strong>
topicSpecific.hint: The tags must close in the reverse order they were opened.
topicSpecific.answer: <p><strong>Important text</strong></p>
topicSpecific.explanation: When nesting, close the inner tag first, then the outer tag. The opening order was <p><strong>, so the closing order must be </strong></p>.

topicSpecific.question: Write the HTML boilerplate for a Hindi language page titled 'Namaste Duniya'.
topicSpecific.hint: Use lang="hi" for Hindi.
topicSpecific.answer:
<!DOCTYPE html>
<html lang="hi">
<head>
  <meta charset="UTF-8">
  <meta name="viewport" content="width=device-width, initial-scale=1.0">
  <title>Namaste Duniya</title>
</head>
<body>
  <h1>Namaste Duniya</h1>
</body>
</html>
topicSpecific.explanation: For a Hindi page, use lang="hi" on the html tag. The meta charset UTF-8 is important because it supports Devanagari characters natively.

topicSpecific.question: Which of these should go inside the head and which inside the body: <title>, <h1>, <meta>, <p>?
topicSpecific.hint: Metadata vs visible content.
topicSpecific.answer: Inside head: <title> and <meta>. Inside body: <h1> and <p>.
topicSpecific.explanation: The head holds metadata — information about the page that is not shown on the page. The body holds visible content. <title> shows on the browser tab (technically not 'on the page'), so it belongs in head.

topicSpecific.hint: Check the charset value carefully.
topicSpecific.answer: <meta charset="UTF-8">
topicSpecific.explanation: The charset value must be UTF-8 with a hyphen, not UTF8. Small typos like this can cause special characters to display incorrectly.

topicSpecific.question: Write a complete HTML page with a description meta tag that says 'A personal blog about cricket and coding by Vikram'.
topicSpecific.hint: Use <meta name="description" content="...">
topicSpecific.answer:
<!DOCTYPE html>
<html lang="en">
<head>
  <meta charset="UTF-8">
  <meta name="viewport" content="width=device-width, initial-scale=1.0">
  <meta name="description" content="A personal blog about cricket and coding by Vikram">
  <title>Vikram's Blog</title>
</head>
<body>
  <h1>Welcome to my blog</h1>
</body>
</html>
topicSpecific.explanation: The description meta tag is used by Google and other search engines to show a preview of your page in search results. Write a clear, interesting description in 150-160 characters.

topicSpecific.question:
Will this HTML display the word 'hidden'?
<p>Visible</p>
<!-- <p>hidden</p> -->
topicSpecific.hint: Anything between <!-- and --> is ignored.
topicSpecific.answer: No. Only 'Visible' will be shown. The second paragraph is inside an HTML comment.
topicSpecific.explanation: HTML comments are completely ignored by the browser. Even though the second line contains a valid <p> tag, it is wrapped in <!-- -->, so the browser treats it as a comment and does not render it.

topicSpecific.hint: A meta tag is missing.
topicSpecific.answer: Add the viewport meta tag: <meta name="viewport" content="width=device-width, initial-scale=1.0">
topicSpecific.explanation: Without the viewport meta tag, phones display web pages zoomed out as if they were desktop pages. Adding this tag makes the page adapt to the phone's actual screen width.

topicSpecific.question: What is the difference between <title> and <h1>?
topicSpecific.hint: One is on the tab, the other is on the page.
topicSpecific.answer: <title> shows on the browser tab (inside head). <h1> is a visible heading on the page itself (inside body). They serve different purposes.
topicSpecific.explanation: The title is metadata used by browsers, search engines, and social media. The h1 is visible content on the page. A page can have different text in title vs h1, though they are often related.

topicSpecific.question: Write a complete HTML page that has two sections, each marked with an HTML comment explaining what it contains.
topicSpecific.hint: Use <!-- --> comments above each section.
topicSpecific.answer:
<!DOCTYPE html>
<html lang="en">
<head>
  <meta charset="UTF-8">
  <meta name="viewport" content="width=device-width, initial-scale=1.0">
  <title>Two Sections</title>
</head>
<body>
  <!-- Introduction section -->
  <h1>About Me</h1>
  <p>I am learning HTML.</p>

  <!-- Hobbies section -->
  <h2>My Hobbies</h2>
  <p>I enjoy reading and cricket.</p>
</body>
</html>
topicSpecific.explanation: Comments help organise larger HTML pages. Professionals often add comments above each major section to make the code easier to navigate.

topicSpecific.hint: Look at the comment boundaries.
topicSpecific.answer: Both paragraphs are inside a multi-line comment. Remove the comment markers to show them, or move the closing --> to hide only the first paragraph.
topicSpecific.explanation: HTML comments can span multiple lines. Everything between <!-- and the next --> is ignored. In this example, both paragraphs are inside the comment, so neither is visible.

topicSpecific.question: Write a complete HTML page with: proper boilerplate, author meta tag crediting 'Ananya Patel', description meta tag, title 'Ananya's Portfolio', and a body with an h1 and a short paragraph.
topicSpecific.hint: Combine all the meta tags you have learned so far.
topicSpecific.answer:
<!DOCTYPE html>
<html lang="en">
<head>
  <meta charset="UTF-8">
  <meta name="viewport" content="width=device-width, initial-scale=1.0">
  <meta name="description" content="Portfolio of Ananya Patel featuring art, writing, and coding projects.">
  <meta name="author" content="Ananya Patel">
  <title>Ananya's Portfolio</title>
</head>
<body>
  <h1>Welcome to my portfolio</h1>
  <p>Here you will find my art, stories, and small code projects.</p>
</body>
</html>
topicSpecific.explanation: This boilerplate includes all common meta tags: charset, viewport, description, and author. The description is what shows up in Google search results, and the author tag credits the creator.

topicSpecific.hint: Think about how the browser renders whitespace in HTML source.
topicSpecific.answer: There is no visual difference. Both render as a bulleted list with two items. HTML ignores extra whitespace in the source code.
topicSpecific.explanation: HTML collapses whitespace when rendering — multiple spaces, tabs, and newlines in the source become a single space (or nothing) on the page. Indentation is only for readability, not for layout.

topicSpecific.hint: Missing exclamation, missing quotes, unclosed title.
topicSpecific.answer: 1. <DOCTYPE html> should be <!DOCTYPE html> (missing !). 2. lang=en should be lang="en" (missing quotes). 3. <title>Test is missing its closing tag. Charset value should also be "UTF-8" with quotes and hyphen.
topicSpecific.explanation: This is a common beginner boilerplate riddled with small mistakes. The exclamation mark is essential for DOCTYPE. Attribute values should always be in quotes. Every tag with content (like title) needs a closing tag.

topicSpecific.question: Write a fully valid HTML boilerplate with a comment section header above every major block in the body. Include intro, hobbies, and contact sections.
topicSpecific.hint: Structure with clear comments above each h2 section.
topicSpecific.answer:
<!DOCTYPE html>
<html lang="en">
<head>
  <meta charset="UTF-8">
  <meta name="viewport" content="width=device-width, initial-scale=1.0">
  <title>About Priya</title>
</head>
<body>
  <!-- ===== Intro ===== -->
  <h1>Priya Sharma</h1>
  <p>Hi, I am a student and hobbyist coder.</p>

  <!-- ===== Hobbies ===== -->
  <h2>Hobbies</h2>
  <p>Reading, painting, and HTML.</p>

  <!-- ===== Contact ===== -->
  <h2>Contact</h2>
  <p>Email: priya@example.com</p>
</body>
</html>
topicSpecific.explanation: Using section-header comments like this makes longer HTML files much easier to navigate. Professional developers often use visual dividers like === inside comments to make sections stand out.

mixed.question: Which tag wraps ALL other HTML tags?
mixed.explanation: The <html> element is the root of every HTML document. It wraps both <head> and <body>. Only the DOCTYPE declaration comes before it.

mixed.question: Write a comment that says 'Footer starts here'.
mixed.explanation: HTML comments begin with <!-- and end with -->. They are invisible on the page and useful for marking sections.

mixed.question:
What is wrong here?
<html><body><h1>Hi</h1></body></html>
mixed.answer: The <!DOCTYPE html> declaration and the <head> section (with title and meta tags) are missing.
mixed.explanation: Technically browsers may still render this, but a proper HTML page needs a DOCTYPE and a head section with at least a title and meta tags.

mixed.question: Where does <meta charset="UTF-8"> go?
mixed.explanation: All meta tags go inside the head. They are metadata — information about the page that is not visible on the page itself.

mixed.question: Write an HTML page with a title 'Bakery' and body containing a heading 'Welcome to our Bakery'.
mixed.hint: Standard boilerplate with these two text values.
mixed.answer:
<!DOCTYPE html>
<html lang="en">
<head>
  <meta charset="UTF-8">
  <meta name="viewport" content="width=device-width, initial-scale=1.0">
  <title>Bakery</title>
</head>
<body>
  <h1>Welcome to our Bakery</h1>
</body>
</html>
mixed.explanation: Standard boilerplate with 'Bakery' as the title (shown on browser tab) and 'Welcome to our Bakery' as the main heading inside body (shown on the page).

mixed.hint: Paragraphs should not be inside a list — and list items should be direct children of the list.
mixed.explanation: List items (<li>) must be direct children of <ul> or <ol>. Wrapping them in paragraphs is invalid HTML.

mixed.question: Does the order of meta tags in the head matter?
mixed.hint: Think about the charset tag in particular.
mixed.answer: Yes. The <meta charset> tag should be among the first things in the head (within the first 1024 bytes) so the browser knows how to read the rest of the file.
mixed.explanation: The charset tag must appear very early because the browser needs to know the character encoding before reading other tags. A good order is: charset, viewport, title, other meta tags.

mixed.question: Write an HTML boilerplate that includes a comment explaining what each meta tag does.
mixed.hint: Add a comment above each meta tag.
mixed.answer:
<!DOCTYPE html>
<html lang="en">
<head>
  <!-- Character encoding for all languages -->
  <meta charset="UTF-8">
  <!-- Make the page mobile friendly -->
  <meta name="viewport" content="width=device-width, initial-scale=1.0">
  <title>Documented Page</title>
</head>
<body>
</body>
</html>
mixed.explanation: Adding comments like this is useful when you are learning — it helps you remember what each tag does. You can remove them later for cleaner production code.

mixed.answer: The closing </head> tag is missing. Add it before <body>.
mixed.explanation: Every tag that has content needs an opening and a closing tag. The head section should end with </head> before the body begins.

mixed.question:
Is this HTML valid?
<!DOCTYPE HTML>
mixed.hint: HTML is not case-sensitive for tags.
mixed.answer: Yes. HTML is not case-sensitive, so <!DOCTYPE HTML> is valid, but <!DOCTYPE html> (lowercase) is the standard convention.
mixed.explanation: HTML tag names and attributes are case-insensitive. Both <!DOCTYPE html> and <!DOCTYPE HTML> work. However, lowercase is the modern convention and what most developers use.

mixed.question: Write a complete HTML page where the title and h1 are different: title should be 'Priya's Blog | Home' and h1 should be 'Welcome to my blog'.
mixed.hint: Title goes in head, h1 goes in body.
mixed.answer:
<!DOCTYPE html>
<html lang="en">
<head>
  <meta charset="UTF-8">
  <meta name="viewport" content="width=device-width, initial-scale=1.0">
  <title>Priya's Blog | Home</title>
</head>
<body>
  <h1>Welcome to my blog</h1>
</body>
</html>
mixed.explanation: The title in the head is what shows on the browser tab and in search results. The h1 in the body is what users see on the page itself. They can have completely different text, though they should be related.

mixed.hint: What kind of content belongs inside <title>?
mixed.answer: The <title> tag only accepts plain text, not other HTML tags. Remove the <h1> from inside the title. Correct version: <title>Hi</title>.
mixed.explanation: The title element is unusual — it only contains text, not other tags. Browsers do not render HTML tags inside title. Always use plain text in title.

mixed.question: Build a complete HTML page with all the following: DOCTYPE, html with lang, head containing charset, viewport, description, author, title, and body containing a section-header comment and an h1 with 'Complete Page'.
mixed.hint: Include every meta tag and a comment in the body.
mixed.answer:
<!DOCTYPE html>
<html lang="en">
<head>
  <meta charset="UTF-8">
  <meta name="viewport" content="width=device-width, initial-scale=1.0">
  <meta name="description" content="A complete example page.">
  <meta name="author" content="Aarav Kumar">
  <title>Complete Page</title>
</head>
<body>
  <!-- Main content -->
  <h1>Complete Page</h1>
</body>
</html>
mixed.explanation: This is a full, production-ready HTML boilerplate with all the common meta tags. Every professional web page starts with something very close to this.

mixed.question:
Why does this comment not work?
<!-- This is -- a bad -- comment -->
mixed.hint: Something special about double dashes inside comments.
mixed.answer: HTML comments cannot contain -- (two dashes) inside them. Use single dashes or other symbols instead: <!-- This is - a better - comment -->
mixed.explanation: The HTML spec says comments cannot contain -- (two consecutive dashes) anywhere except at the start and end. This can cause validation errors and strange parsing issues.

mixed.answer: The browser is very forgiving. It will likely close the <h1> automatically at the </body> and still show 'Hello' as a heading. But this is invalid HTML and may cause issues in complex pages.
mixed.explanation: Modern browsers try hard to fix broken HTML automatically. However, you should always close your tags properly. Browsers may fix simple mistakes, but complex nesting errors can still cause layouts to break.

mcqs.question: What should be the very first line of every HTML5 page?

options.B: <head>

mcqs.explanation: C is correct. <!DOCTYPE html> must be the very first line. It tells the browser this is an HTML5 document.

mcqs.question: Which tag contains the visible content of the page?

options.B: <body>

mcqs.explanation: B is correct. The <body> element contains everything the user sees — headings, paragraphs, images, and more.

mcqs.question: Where does the <title> tag go?

options.B: Inside <head>
options.D: Inside <html> but outside head and body

mcqs.explanation: B is correct. The <title> tag goes inside <head>. It sets the text shown on the browser tab.

mcqs.question: Which character encoding should you use?
mcqs.explanation: C is correct. UTF-8 is the universal encoding that supports every language and symbol, including Hindi, Tamil, emoji, and more.

mcqs.question: Which is the correct syntax for an HTML comment?

options.B: /* This is a comment */

mcqs.explanation: C is correct. HTML comments use <!-- -->. The other syntaxes are from JavaScript, CSS, and Python.

mcqs.question: Which tag wraps the head and body of an HTML page?

options.B: <document>

mcqs.explanation: C is correct. The <html> element is the root of every HTML page. It wraps both the head and the body.

mcqs.question: What is metadata?

options.B: Information about the page that is not visible on the page itself
options.D: The main content of the page

mcqs.explanation: B is correct. Metadata means 'data about data'. In HTML, metadata (like title, charset, description) describes the page but is not displayed as visible content.

mcqs.question: What does the viewport meta tag do?

options.B: Makes the page look right on mobile devices

mcqs.explanation: B is correct. The viewport meta tag tells mobile browsers to display the page at the device's actual width, not zoomed out.

mcqs.question: What does the lang attribute on <html> do?

options.B: Sets the page's human language for screen readers and SEO

mcqs.explanation: B is correct. lang="en" tells browsers and screen readers the page is in English. It helps accessibility and search engines.

mcqs.question: What is wrong with <meta charset="UTF8">?

options.B: UTF8 should be UTF-8 with a hyphen
options.C: Should be charset-type instead of charset

mcqs.explanation: B is correct. The correct value is UTF-8 with a hyphen. While some browsers may accept UTF8, the standard is UTF-8.

mcqs.question: Which of these is valid nesting?

options.B: Hello

mcqs.explanation: B is correct. Tags must close in the reverse order they were opened. Opening <b><i> means closing </i></b>.

mcqs.question: Why should you indent nested HTML?

options.B: It changes how the page looks
options.C: It makes the code easier for humans to read

mcqs.explanation: C is correct. Indentation is purely for readability. HTML ignores whitespace, so indentation has no effect on how the page looks — but it makes your code much easier to understand.

mcqs.question: Which meta tag helps search engines show a preview?

options.B: <meta name="viewport">

mcqs.explanation: C is correct. <meta name="description"> provides the text shown in Google search results below the blue link.

mcqs.question: Where should comments be used in HTML?

options.A: To store passwords and private data
options.B: To explain sections of code for yourself and other developers
options.C: To display help text to users

mcqs.explanation: B is correct. Comments are for developers, not users. Never store private information in comments — anyone can view the page source.

mcqs.question: Without the DOCTYPE, what happens?

options.B: The browser falls back to 'quirks mode' which breaks modern CSS

mcqs.explanation: B is correct. Without DOCTYPE, browsers use 'quirks mode' which emulates ancient buggy HTML rendering. This can break CSS layouts in unpredictable ways.

mcqs.question: What is the correct order inside the head?

options.B: meta charset, viewport, title

mcqs.explanation: B is correct. The charset should come first so the browser knows how to decode the rest. Then viewport, then title. This order is considered best practice.

mcqs.question: Can you put an <h1> tag inside a <title> tag?

options.A: Yes, and it will show as a big heading
options.B: Yes, but it is ignored and the tags show as text
options.C: No, title only contains plain text

mcqs.explanation: C is correct. The title element only accepts plain text. Other HTML tags inside it are either ignored or show as literal text in the browser tab.

mcqs.question: What does a multi-line HTML comment look like?

options.B:
<!-- Comment on
multiple lines -->
options.D: HTML does not support multi-line comments

mcqs.explanation: B is correct. HTML comments can span multiple lines. Just put <!-- at the start and --> at the end, and include any content between them.

mcqs.question: Is HTML case-sensitive for tag names?

options.A: Yes, all tags must be lowercase
options.B: Yes, all tags must be uppercase
options.C: No, but lowercase is the modern convention

mcqs.explanation: C is correct. HTML tags are case-insensitive — <P> and <p> both work. However, lowercase is the standard modern practice.

mcqs.question: What happens if you put content directly inside <html> but outside both head and body?

options.A: It appears at the top of the page
options.B: The browser tries to move it into the body automatically

mcqs.explanation: B is correct. Browsers are forgiving. They usually move stray content into the body automatically. But this is invalid HTML and you should never rely on it.

codingChallenges.title: Write the Perfect Boilerplate
codingChallenges.question: Write the complete HTML5 boilerplate with title 'My Clean Page' and a single h1 in the body saying 'Clean and proper'.
codingChallenges.expectedOutput: A page with proper structure: DOCTYPE, html (with lang), head (with charset, viewport, title), and body (with h1).
codingChallenges.constraints: Must include DOCTYPE, lang attribute, meta charset, meta viewport, title, and a single h1.
codingChallenges.solution: Clean and proper
codingChallenges.explanation: This is the minimum valid HTML5 boilerplate. Memorise this structure — you will use it on every single HTML file you ever write.
codingChallenges.alternativeSolution: Clean and proper

codingChallenges.title: Commented Sections
codingChallenges.question: Build an HTML page with three sections in the body — Introduction, Hobbies, Contact — each labelled with an HTML comment above the section's h2 heading.
codingChallenges.expectedOutput: A page with three h2 sections, each preceded by a comment like <!-- Introduction -->.
codingChallenges.constraints: Include DOCTYPE, proper head, and three h2 sections with comments.
codingChallenges.solution:
Introduction

Hi, I am Ananya and I love coding.

Hobbies

Reading, painting, and learning HTML.

Contact

Email: ananya@example.com
codingChallenges.explanation: Comments like this help you navigate longer HTML files. They are invisible to users but make your code much easier to read and maintain.
codingChallenges.alternativeSolution:
Introduction

About me.

Hobbies

My interests.

Contact

Reach me here.

codingChallenges.title: Multilingual Ready Boilerplate
codingChallenges.question: Create an HTML page for a Hindi welcome message. Use lang="hi", UTF-8 charset, and include a heading 'Namaste Duniya' and a paragraph 'HTML mein Hindi aasan hai.'
codingChallenges.expectedOutput: A page with Hindi-ready setup: lang="hi", UTF-8 charset, and Hindi-appropriate text.
codingChallenges.constraints: Must use lang="hi" on html tag and UTF-8 charset to support Devanagari.
codingChallenges.solution:
Namaste Duniya

HTML mein Hindi aasan hai.
codingChallenges.explanation: Setting lang="hi" tells browsers and screen readers the page is in Hindi. UTF-8 charset supports Devanagari and other Indian scripts.
codingChallenges.alternativeSolution:
Namaste Duniya

HTML mein Hindi aasan hai.

codingChallenges.title: SEO Ready Page
codingChallenges.question: Build a complete HTML page with description and author meta tags. Title should be 'Priya's Art Portfolio', description should describe an art portfolio, author should be 'Priya Sharma'. Include a simple heading and paragraph in the body.
codingChallenges.expectedOutput: A page with title, description, author meta tags, and basic body content.
codingChallenges.constraints: Include all SEO meta tags: charset, viewport, description, author, title.
codingChallenges.solution:
Welcome to my art portfolio

Explore my collection of paintings and sketches.
codingChallenges.explanation: The description meta tag appears in search engine results below the blue link. The author meta tag credits the creator. Both help SEO (Search Engine Optimisation).
codingChallenges.alternativeSolution:
Priya's Art

Welcome to my creative space.

codingChallenges.title: Fix the Broken Boilerplate
codingChallenges.question: This HTML has five mistakes. Rewrite the whole page correctly. Test <h1>Hello</h1> </head> <body> </body>
codingChallenges.expectedOutput: A fully corrected HTML boilerplate with all five issues fixed.
codingChallenges.constraints: Fix DOCTYPE, lang, charset, title closing, and content placement. Also add viewport meta.
codingChallenges.solution: Hello
codingChallenges.explanation: Fixes: (1) DOCTYPE needs the exclamation mark, (2) lang attribute needs quotes, (3) charset should be UTF-8 with hyphen and in quotes, (4) title was missing its closing tag, (5) h1 was inside head — moved to body. Also added the missing viewport meta tag and closing </html>.
codingChallenges.alternativeSolution: Hello

codingChallenges.title: Nested Structure Page
codingChallenges.question: Create an HTML page that shows a menu with a nested list. The main list has 'Snacks' and 'Drinks' as categories, and each category contains a sub-list of two items. Use proper indentation.
codingChallenges.expectedOutput: A menu page with a nested bulleted list showing categories and items.
codingChallenges.constraints: Proper nesting and indentation required. Include full boilerplate.
codingChallenges.solution:
Our Menu

  • Snacks
    • Samosa
    • Vada Pav
  • Drinks
    • Masala Chai
    • Mango Lassi
codingChallenges.explanation: Nested lists use a <ul> inside an <li>. The browser automatically indents the sub-list. Notice how each nesting level is indented by 2 spaces — this makes the code much easier to read.
codingChallenges.alternativeSolution:
Menu

  • Snacks
    • Samosa
    • Vada Pav
  • Drinks
    • Chai
    • Lassi

codingChallenges.title: Complete Blog Post Template
codingChallenges.question: Build a complete HTML page that serves as a blog post template. Include full boilerplate with description and author meta tags, and in the body add section-labeled comments for the header, content, and footer. Use h1, p, and hr tags appropriately.
codingChallenges.expectedOutput: A professional blog post HTML template with proper meta tags and commented sections.
codingChallenges.constraints: Include DOCTYPE, head with charset, viewport, description, author, title, and body with commented header, content, footer sections.
codingChallenges.solution:
My First Blog Post

By Rohan Gupta

Learning HTML has been one of the best decisions I have made this year.

The boilerplate looked intimidating at first, but now I can type it out without thinking.

Thanks for reading. See you in the next post.
codingChallenges.explanation: This is a real blog post template you could actually use. It has proper SEO meta tags, a clear structure with header/content/footer comments, and uses hr tags to visually separate sections.
codingChallenges.alternativeSolution:
Post Title

By Neha Patel

Post content paragraph one.

Post content paragraph two.

End of post.

codingChallenges.title: Full SEO and Mobile Ready Home Page
codingChallenges.question: Build a complete home page that is SEO friendly and mobile ready. Include DOCTYPE, html with lang, complete head (charset, viewport, description, author, title), and body with a commented header section, a commented main section with h1 and two paragraphs, and a commented footer section.
codingChallenges.expectedOutput: A fully complete, professional-quality HTML home page.
codingChallenges.constraints: Must be SEO ready (description, proper title), mobile ready (viewport meta), and well-organized with section comments.
codingChallenges.solution:
Welcome to my site

Hi, I am Vikram, a 15-year-old from Pune who loves building websites.

This site is where I share my coding journey, projects, and thoughts about technology.

Feel free to explore and reach out if you want to collaborate on something fun.

Made with love and plain HTML by Vikram Singh.
codingChallenges.explanation: This is a production-quality home page. It has every best practice: proper DOCTYPE, language attribute, charset, viewport, description for SEO, author credit, clear title, organised body with section comments, and a footer. This is exactly how professional web pages are structured.
codingChallenges.alternativeSolution:
Hi, I am Aarav

A student and coder from Chennai.

I build small web projects for fun.

Always learning something new.

Thanks for visiting.
